channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.eurogamer.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.eurogamer.net

item.title: Professor Layton and the New World of Steam gets a release window and adds PS5 and PC versions
item.content:encoded:
After an early 2023 reveal, Professor Layton and the New World of Steam - the next entry in the long-running puzzle adventure series - hasn't been making much noise. Today's Level-5 showcase event has changed that with a new in-depth look at the game.

But the juiciest bits of information come from an accompanying press release. First: the game is on its way to release later in 2026. And second: many players will be happy to learn Professor Layton and the New World of Steam will also launch on PS5 and PC (Steam), alongside the previously revealed Nintendo Switch 1 & 2 versions.

The shift to multiplatform doesn't exactly come as a shock after Fantasy Life i: The Girl Who Steals Time delivered a huge win for Level-5 last year, with more than 1.5m sales across PC, PS4/5, Xbox Series X/S, and Nintendo Switch 1/2. Also, the Professor Layton games are traditionally associated with Nintendo's handhelds and mobile phones, so PlayStation and PC versions an exciting development.

Key features in New World of Steam include puzzle design by the riddles and quizzes experts of QuizKnock, a full world map, updated movement, "puzzles that help the town grow", new companions, full 3D cutscenes, Switch 2 mouse support, and a "coin radar" mode. The new setting is Steam Bison, a town in America powered by revolutionary steam technology that rivals the development seen in London. There, Layton and Luke team up one year after the events of Professor Layton and the Unwound Future.

Level-5 also confirmed language support has been expanded, with English, French, Italian, German, Spanish, Japanese, Korean, Traditional Chinese, and Simplified Chinese available at launch. No word yet on a possible English dub, but the Japanese voice cast was revealed today too.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/professor-layton-and-the-new-world-of-steam-pc-ps5-release-window
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Datamined code shows Valve referencing an AI "SteamGPT" tool presumably to help Steam better cope with cheaters and customer support
item.content:encoded:
So far there's only been hints that game-maker and Steam-owner Valve has been dabbling in generative AI use. But if recent findings are to be believed, it might be working on a "SteamGPT" AI feature to help tackle cheating on Steam and manage account restrictions.

Valve-focused dataminer Gabe Follower (via Tom's Hardware), who traditionally works through Steam code updates looking for new information, unearthed code recently that mentioned "SteamGPT" and did so in relation to account statistics and Steam's Trust Score metric. This helps Valve deal with cheating and account restrictions in online games connected to Steam.

It seems that Valve is working on a "SteamGPT" feature that will apparently deal with Steam support issues and is somehow connected to Trust Score and CS2 anti-cheat? pic.twitter.com/a3MckicQf2

— ‎Gabe Follower (@gabefollower) April 7, 2026

To see this content please enable targeting cookies.

As pointed out by Gabe Follower, the code appears to be tied to several account stats such as age, confidence score, model evaluation, and even pre-existing bans as variables. If Valve is indeed working on an AI helper, it seems the goal here is to improve the efficiency of the process of dealing with customer support tickers and recurring issues like false bans.

Valve has struggled with cheaters on Steam before, and its technology hasn't always been up to the task. In late 2023, Valve's Anti-Cheat (VAC) System doled out false bans for simply moving your mouse too quickly in Counter-Strike 2. Meanwhile, cheating continues to be an issue that Valve is battling against, so AI automation could be appealing, even if the divisive tech continues to raise concerns.

Nevertheless, this unearthed code could only be something Valve is testing but won't release. And even if it does make it through to release, "SteamGPT" might remain an internal tool rather than anything consumer-facing.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/steam-code-references-steamgpt
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: After selling over 1.5m copies and stealing most of my summer, Fantasy Life i is coming to mobile with cross-save
item.content:encoded:
Fantasy Life i: The Girl Who Steals Time was one of last year's most pleasant surprises, successfully managing to suck up most of my summer with its brilliant mix of Animal Crossing-style life sim and swashbuckling RPG adventure. And now it's coming to iOS and Android with cross-save, meaning you can take your existing games with you on-the-go.

Developed by Professor Layton studio Level-5, Fantasy Life i: The Girl Who Steals Time is the second outing for the Fantasy Life series, which originated on Nintendo 3DS all the way back in 2012. Despite almost a decade between instalments, the fundamentals remain the same, meaning you're essentially playing as a classic RPG hero - facing the usual world-ending calamity - but one who needs to get a job to pave their way. Or jobs as the case may be.

In Fantasy Life i, time and space are wonky, and you'll be bounding all over the place. Back in the past, you learn new trades, becoming a trainee mercenary, perhaps, or a paladin, a hunter, a magician, an angler, a miner, a farmer, a woodcutter, a cook, a blacksmith, a carpenter, and more. Or - more likely - a whole bunch of these things. And as you improve, you'll learn crucial skills that'll help during your adventure. To fight monsters! To grow food! To craft exotic new weapons! To mine for the ore to create exotic new weapons! Etc.

The list of activities is vast, but the game itself is even vaster. The past plays host to numerous islands full of friends to make and dungeons to explore - or you can pop through a portal to explore a massive open world full of enchanted objects you can turn back into humans who'll join your party and fight by your side. And over in the present, there's a whole Animal Crossing-style side game going on, where you'll build a village for your new friends to live in. So you can probably start to see why its more-ish loops of crafting and scavenging and levelling and building and adventuring hoovered up so much of my time on Switch 2. And given Fantasy Life i had sold 1.5m copies by the time December came around, I doubt I was the only one.

Which brings us back to Level-5's announcement of an Android and iOS release for Fantasy Life i. It's arriving as a premium title rather than a micro-transaction-riddled free-to-play effort, and all the DLC and updates available on other platforms are included. Additionally, Level-5 says it features touch controls and full controller support. Even better, cross-saves and cross-play are supported, meaning you can hop between platforms, taking your current progress to the beach, office loo, or other destination of your choosing.

The only thing we don't have right now is a launch date, but Level-5 is aiming to bring Fantasy Life i to iOS and Android this "summer".

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/fantasy-life-i-is-coming-to-mobile-with-cross-save
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
